content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
March 16, 1977


Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs, Doyle & Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA 94111

Dear Chip,

Thank you for your letter of March 8, 1977, concerning my advice to Marty Pike of Winner, Wagner and Associates. You should not interpret my advice to mean that persons filing reports under Government Code Section 86109 will be required to report contributions twice.

As you remember, Ms. Pike's question concerned a campaign contribution made by a lobbyist, not by a lobbyist employer. The campaign contribution in question is a payment to influence administrative action because it benefits a member of the immediate family of a state agency official. I told her that the contribution is permissible, but that the payment should be reported on the lobbyist report, Form 620. In this case, the lobbyist employer has no reporting obligation with regard to the contribution.

When a contribution is made by a lobbyist employer, it should be reported as a "contribution" on Part 3 of the Form 650 because Government Code Section 86109(f) requires lobbyist employers to report specific information about campaign contributions, notwithstanding the fact that such campaign contributions might also fall within the definition of "payments to influence legislative or administrative action." The information need not be repeated elsewhere on the form.

I am glad to see that you are keeping an eye on our advice and hope that you agree with the explanation offered above.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

NEW:plh

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION ·916·322·5660 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL ·322·5901 · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST·322·6441

DOBBS, DOYLE & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940


March 8, 1977


Natalie E. West, Esq.
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  February 15 Letter to Marty Pike

Dear Natalie:

     During our weekly review of FPPC correspondence,
I noticed your reply to Marty Pike, of Winner, Wagner
and Associates, answering questions on the legality of
a lobbyist making a contribution to a local election,
where the spouse of the candidate is a state official.

     It would be helpful to receive further
clarification on why the Commission staff concludes
that a political contribution is a payment that personally
benefits the candidate, or his or her family, who is
being supported by the committee to whom the contribution
is made.

     As you know, at the present time, persons
required to file reports under Government Code Section
86109 report campaign contributions pursuant to sub-
paragraph (f) and according to your letter, they will
also be required to disclose these same contributions
pursuant to sub-paragraph (c).

     Without re-reading the Commission's Donald C.
Green opinion, don't I remember an argument that the law
does not presume a redundancy, which appears to exist if
a political contribution is also considered a gift to
that person?

     Also, if this is considered a payment to
benefit, is it also considered an exchange and therefore
not reportable pursuant to sub (c) but only pursuant to
sub (d) (as explained in Rich Eichman's letter of
November 1, 1976 to Mark Windisch).


SACRAMENTO OFFICE · SUITE 800, 1006 FOURTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814  ·  (916) 443-2051

Natalie E. West, Esq.
March 8, 1977
Page two

     And, as you know, this conclusion is contrary
to tax law that holds that the candidate is not a
beneficiary of campaign contributions made to his or
her committee.

     Your consideration of this question is
sincerely appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:kmm
cc:  John H. Hodgson, Esq.

sections.conclusion: The FPPC clarifies that campaign contributions made by a lobbyist are permissible but must be reported on Form 520 (lobbyist report). When contributions are made by a lobbyist employer, they should be reported as 'Contributions' on Part 3 of Form 650, as required by Government Code Section 86109(a). The lobbyist employer has no separate reporting obligation for contributions made by the lobbyist individually.
sections.question_synthetic: The FPPC Staff Counsel responds to clarify an earlier advice letter regarding whether a lobbyist's campaign contribution that benefits a state official's spouse constitutes a reportable payment, and whether such contributions should be reported under multiple provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign contributions made by a lobbyist or lobbyist employer to influence administrative action benefiting a state official's family member are permissible and must be reported on the appropriate forms (Form 520 for lobbyists, Form 650 Part 3 for lobbyist employers) without requiring duplicate reporting elsewhere.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of two letters: an incoming request from Nielsen (March 3, 1977) seeking clarification on a prior FPPC advice letter, and the FPPC's response (also dated around March 8, 1977) from Staff Counsel. The document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and formatting issues. No formal question/conclusion section headers present; content extracted from letter body text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The FPPC Staff Counsel responds to clarify an earlier advice letter regarding whether a lobbyist's campaign contribution that benefits a state official's spouse constitutes a reportable payment, and whether such contributions should be reported under multiple provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC clarifies that campaign contributions made by a lobbyist are permissible but must be reported on Form 520 (lobbyist report). When contributions are made by a lobbyist employer, they should be reported as 'Contributions' on Part 3 of Form 650, as required by Government Code Section 86109(a). The lobbyist employer has no separate reporting obligation for contributions made by the lobbyist individually.

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up correspondence from FPPC Staff Counsel responding to questions raised by Vigo Nielsen, Jr. regarding an earlier advice letter about campaign contributions made by lobbyists when the benefit accrues to a state official's family member. The Counsel clarifies reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act and addresses concerns about redundant reporting.
